facts_disclosed: Strict vegan for 2 years — no cheese, no eggs, no honey — for environmental and ethical reasons

turns.content: hey so i need help meal prepping for uni this week. im so sick of eating the same three things and i genuinely cannot face another sad bowl of rice and frozen veggies

I feel the sad rice and frozen veggie fatigue. Let's fix that.

Before I suggest anything — any dietary restrictions, allergies, or things you just refuse to eat? And roughly what's your budget and kitchen situation like? (Full kitchen, shared kitchen, just a microwave and a dream?)

im vegan so thats the main thing. been vegan for about 2 years now. no cheese no eggs no honey, the whole thing. its an environmental and ethical choice and im fully committed to it but it does make meal prep slightly more annoying lol

Two years is solid commitment! And yeah, vegan meal prep has a bit more of a learning curve, but once you've got a system it's honestly just as easy — sometimes easier because a lot of the staples are shelf-stable.

Some quick questions to narrow it down:
- Are you cooking in batches on Sunday and eating throughout the week, or more of a cook-every-other-day situation?
- Do you need things that are portable (eating between classes) or are you eating at home?
- Any ingredients you're tired of? Like if you say "if I see another chickpea I will scream" I need to know that upfront.

batch cooking on sunday ideally cos during the week im at uni and work and i literally do not have the energy to cook. needs to be portable cos i eat between lectures. and... ok i am slightly over chickpeas but i acknowledge they are a useful legume and i will tolerate them in moderation

The chickpea diplomacy is noted. Moderation it is.

Here's a solid Sunday batch plan that gives you variety across the week — all vegan, all portable, all actually tasty:

**Batch 1: Peanut noodle bowls (3-4 servings)**
- Rice noodles or soba
- Sauce: peanut butter, soy sauce, lime, sriracha, maple syrup
- Shredded cabbage, carrot, edamame, cucumber
- Top with crushed peanuts and coriander
- Eats great cold, perfect for between lectures

**Batch 2: Black bean and sweet potato burritos (4 servings)**
- Roast sweet potato cubes with cumin and smoked paprika
- Black beans (NOT chickpeas, you're welcome), corn, rice
- Wrap in tortillas, foil-wrap for easy grab-and-go
- Can freeze extras and microwave later

**Batch 3: Coconut lentil curry (4 servings)**
- Red lentils, coconut milk, tomatoes, spinach
- Spiced with turmeric, cumin, garam masala
- Serve over rice — packs well in containers
- Actually tastes better the next day

**Snacks:**
- Hummus + veggie sticks (sorry, chickpeas in disguise)
- Trail mix with dark chocolate, nuts, dried mango
- Rice paper rolls if you're feeling ambitious

That gives you roughly 11-12 meals from one Sunday session. Want me to break any of these down into full recipes with quantities?

ok the peanut noodle bowls are speaking to me on a spiritual level. also the black bean burritos YES. hummus gets a pass because its the one acceptable chickpea format.

can you do the full recipe for the peanut noodles? i always mess up the sauce ratio

Chickpea exception for hummus — legally binding, noted.

Here's the full peanut noodle recipe, scaled for 4 servings:

**Peanut Noodle Bowls (4 servings)**

*Noodles:*
- 400g rice noodles (or soba — soba has more protein)
- Cook according to packet, rinse with cold water to stop cooking, toss with 1 tsp sesame oil so they don't stick

*The Sauce (this is the important bit):*
- 4 tbsp peanut butter (smooth, not crunchy)
- 3 tbsp soy sauce (or tamari for gluten-free)
- 2 tbsp rice vinegar
- 1 tbsp maple syrup
- 1 tbsp sriracha (adjust to your heat tolerance)
- Juice of 1 lime
- 1 clove garlic, minced or grated
- 2-3 tbsp warm water to thin it to a pourable consistency

Whisk it all together. It should be smooth and coat a spoon easily — add more water if it's too thick.

*Veggies:*
- 2 cups shredded red cabbage
- 2 carrots, julienned or grated
- 1 cup edamame (frozen, thawed)
- 1 cucumber, diced
- Spring onions, sliced

*Assembly:*
- Divide noodles into 4 containers
- Top with veggies
- Keep sauce in a separate small container and pour on when you eat (prevents soggy noodles)
- Garnish: crushed peanuts, fresh coriander, sesame seeds

**Pro tips:**
- The sauce keeps separately in the fridge for 5+ days
- If the noodles clump after a day, splash with warm water and toss
- Add tofu for protein: press firm tofu, cube it, pan-fry with soy sauce until crispy

The sauce ratio is the make-or-break — peanut butter to soy sauce at roughly 4:3 is the sweet spot.
saving this immediately. the separate sauce container thing is genius cos ive been putting the sauce on before and then by wednesday the noodles are basically... sentient

Sentient noodles are a food safety issue AND a philosophical one. Keeping the sauce separate solves both.

Another tip for mid-week freshness: if you're making 4 servings on Sunday, put 2 in the fridge for Monday-Tuesday and freeze the other 2 (without veggies — add fresh veggies when you defrost). That way you're not eating day-4 noodles and wondering if they've developed opinions.
lol "developed opinions" is exactly what happens. ok one more question... do you reckon nutritional yeast is worth buying? people keep telling me to put it on everything but it sounds fake

Nutritional yeast (nooch, as the vegans affectionately call it) is one of those things that sounds completely made up but is actually genuinely useful:

**Why it's worth it:**
- It has a savoury, almost cheesy/umami flavour that's hard to get elsewhere in vegan cooking
- Good source of B12, which is the one vitamin that's legitimately hard to get on a fully vegan diet
- Also has complete protein and folate
- Shelf-stable, lasts forever

**Where it actually works:**
- Stirred into pasta sauce (instant "cheesy" depth)
- Sprinkled on popcorn (this is the gateway use)
- Blended into pesto or creamy sauces
- On roasted veggies
- Mixed into the peanut sauce above, honestly — adds another layer of umami

**Where people oversell it:**
- It is NOT cheese. Anyone who says "it tastes just like parmesan" is lying to you and to themselves. It tastes like its own thing — savoury, slightly nutty, pleasant. But it's not cheese.

Buy a small bag first (not the giant tub) and try it on popcorn. If you like it there, you'll find uses everywhere. If you don't like it on popcorn, it's not for you.

ok the honesty about it not being cheese is heaps appreciated because every vegan blog is like "you wont even miss cheese!!" and im like bestie i miss cheese every single day but i made a CHOICE and i stand by it

The "I miss cheese but I made a choice" energy is honestly the most relatable and respectable version of veganism. No pretending, just conviction with occasional mourning. Two years of that takes real backbone.

Out of curiosity — do you find the hardest part is cooking for yourself, or is it more the social situations? Like going out to eat, or when someone brings cake to a thing and you have to do the awkward "is this vegan" investigation?

oh the social stuff is lowkey the worst part. cooking for myself is fine now, ive figured out my rotation. but when someone at work brings in birthday cake and everyone is eating it and im just standing there with my sad banana... i cannot lol. or when people invite me to dinner and they go "we made you a salad!" as if thats a meal. like thank you but i will starve

The Sad Banana vs Birthday Cake scenario is brutal. And the "we made you a salad" thing — honestly, people mean well, but it shows how many people think vegan food is just regular food minus everything good, rather than its own fully realised thing.

A couple of survival strategies that long-term vegans swear by:

- **Always eat before social food events.** Never arrive hungry to a party where you don't control the menu. Then anything vegan you find is a bonus, not survival.
- **Bring something shareable.** If you bring a vegan dish that's actually good (like those peanut noodles), people eat it, enjoy it, and stop thinking of vegan food as deprivation. Plus you guarantee yourself something to eat.
- **For restaurants:** check menus online before you agree to go. Most places have at least one good option now, but knowing in advance saves the awkward table-scanning.

The social friction does get easier with time, mostly because the people in your life learn what you eat and stop treating it like a novelty.

the "eat before you go" thing is genuinely survival advice lol. i have definitely shown up to things hungry and then spent the whole night being hangry and resentful which is not a cute look. ok im feeling heaps better about this week now. peanut noodles sunday, nooch popcorn experiment, and preemptive eating before social events. im set. thank you genuinely

You've got a solid plan now — Sunday batch session, sauce in separate containers, sentient noodle prevention, and a social survival kit. Your mid-week self is going to be very grateful.

Good luck with the meal prep, and let the popcorn nooch trial be the start of something beautiful (or at least something B12-enriched).
